# Project Charta
## Context and Scope
Describe the context (domain/business area) and topic of the visualization project. Which topical aspects should be addressed? Which question(s) should the data visualization project answer? What are the expected benefits of the project? Where should the visualization product be published (e.g. website, app, intranet)?

Business travel is common in many companies. Employees travel for meetings, conferences, or to work with colleagues in other locations. However, business travel - especially flights - also produces CO₂ emissions, which has an impact on the environment.

In this project, we will analyse and visualize a dataset about business travel. The dataset contains information about trips such as transport mode, travel distance, travel duration, costs, and estimated CO₂ emissions.

The dataset covers business trips from 2017 to early 2026 and seems to represent travel activities within one company with several business units.

The dashboard should help answer questions such as:

- How have travel emissions changed over time?
- Which transport modes produce the most CO₂ emissions?
- Which business units travel the most?
- How are emissions related to travel distance?
- How do different travel options compare in terms of CO₂ emissions and travel duration?

The dashboard should also help users compare different travel options and support decisions about how to travel. For example, if a trip can be done by train instead of plane, the dashboard can show the potential emissions savings.
When the company has a budget for travel, the dashboard can help to decide if the travel is possible to make. 

The visualization product will be available on a website.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria
Describe the objectives **from the client/stakeholder perspective**. For example: "Enable users to understand the distribution of sales across different regions". Try to be as specific as possible, but restrict to the concrete needs formulated by the client/stakeholders. For example: if a dashboard is needed explicitly, then state it here, otherwise leave the form of the visualization product open.

Formulate success criteria (from the client/stakeholder perspective): qualitative objectives and, wherever possible, corresponding quantitative metrics with target values to be achieved within the project. These objectives should directly address the pains and gains identified in the user analysis.

It is also helpful to specify what is explicitly excluded from the project objectives (out of scope).

From a stakeholder perspective, the project aims to:

- Provide a clear visual overview of business travel activities and their CO₂ emissions

- Allow users to explore travel data across categories such as transport mode, travel distance, business unit, and time
- Enable comparisons between different travel options in terms of emissions and travel duration
- Support users in identifying travel patterns and emission-intensive travel behaviour

The final outcome of the project will be an interactive dashboard that enables exploratory analysis of the travel dataset.

Success Criteria
Qualitative success criteria:
- Users can easily understand the relationship between travel behaviour and CO₂ emissions.
- The dashboard provides a clear overview of travel patterns in the dataset.
- Users are able to compare transportation options and recognize differences in emissions and travel duration.
- Users are able to compare transportation options and recognize differences in emissions and travel duration.

Quantitative success criteria:
- The dashboard contains at least five different visualizations presenting key aspects of the dataset.
- Users can filter the data by at least three attributes (e.g., year, transport mode, business unit).
- Test users can identify at least two insights about travel patterns within one minute of interacting with the dashboard.


Out of Scope: The project will not include recommendations for specific travel options or policies. 

## Stakeholder Analysis
List the people involved in and affected by the project. Describe their goals and relationships with each other. Visualisation in the form of a stakeholder map can provide a quick overview.
?????? Séverine Stadler

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"quadrant1Fill": "#ccffcc", "quadrant2Fill": "#cce5ff", "quadrant3Fill": "#ffffcc", "quadrant4Fill": "#ffcccc"}}}%%
quadrantChart
    title Stakeholder Matrix
    x-axis "Low interest" --> "High interest"
    y-axis "Low Power" --> "High Power"
    quadrant-1 "Manage Closely"
    quadrant-2 "Keep Satisfied"
    quadrant-3 "Monitor"
    quadrant-4 "Keep Informed"
    
    "Laura Smith": [0.85, 0.85]
    "Management": [0.65, 0.90]
    "Business Unit Manager": [0.75, 0.70]
    "Project Team": [0.90, 0.60]
    "IT / Web-Admin": [0.25, 0.75]
    "Employee": [0.80, 0.10]

```

## User Analysis
Analyse the target audience of the visualization product and create at least 2 personas with **relevant attributes** such as domain expertise, data literacy, technical environment (devices, screen sizes), frequency of use etc. Amend each persona with the dimensions of the customer profile side in the Value Proposition Canvas @Osterwalder2014:

* **User tasks** (jobs): What tasks are users trying to accomplish? Consider functional jobs (e.g. analysing data, making decisions), social jobs (e.g. reporting to stakeholders, collaborating with colleagues) and emotional jobs (e.g. feeling confident in their decisions).
* **Pains**: What obstacles, risks or undesired outcomes do users currently face? What makes their current approach frustrating or inefficient?
* **Gains**: What outcomes and benefits do users expect or desire? What would exceed their expectations?

Persona 1: Sustainability Manager
Name: Laura Smith
Role: Sustainability Manager
Domain Expertise: High (expert in sustainability and environmental impact)
Data Literacy: Medium (comfortable with data but not an expert)
Technical Environment: Desktop computer with large monitor, occasional use of mobile devices
Frequency of Use: Weekly

User Tasks:
- Monitor and report on the company's CO₂ emissions from business travel
- Identify trends and patterns in travel behaviour and emissions
- prepare reports and presentations for senior management and stakeholders

Pains:
- Difficulty in accessing and analyzing travel data from multiple sources
- Time-consuming process to create reports and visualizations manually
Gains:
- Ability to quickly identify and analyze travel patterns and emissions
- Improved decision-making capabilities based on data-driven insights

Persona 2: Business Unit Manager -ändern! Séverine Stadler
Name: Mark Johnson
Role: Business Unit Manager
Domain Expertise: Medium (knowledgeable about business operations but not an expert in sustainability)
Data Literacy: Low (limited experience with data analysis and visualization)
Technical Environment: Desktop computer with standard office software, limited use of mobile devices
Frequency of Use: Monthly

User Tasks:
- Review and analyze travel data for budgeting and planning purposes
- Communicate travel-related insights to team members and stakeholders

Pains:
- Lack of easy access to consolidated travel data
- Limited ability to create meaningful visualizations without technical expertise
Gains:
- Simplified access to relevant travel information
- Enhanced ability to communicate travel insights effectively

### Persona 2: Business Unit Manager
- **Name**: Mark Johnson
- **Role**: Business Unit Manager
- **Domain Expertise**: High in business operations; Medium in sustainability (growing awareness due to new corporate ESG mandates).
- **Data Literacy**: Medium (comfortable with business KPIs and budget dashboards, but needs pre-processed visual data for quick insights).
- **Technical Environment**: Desktop/Laptop with standard office software, frequent use of mobile devices while on the go.
- **Frequency of Use**: Monthly (for reporting) and ad-hoc (when approving team travel requests).

**User Tasks (Jobs):**

- Manage and balance the unit's financial travel budget and their allocated CO₂ emission budget.
- Align the team's operational needs (e.g., client meetings, conferences) with strict corporate sustainability requirements.
- Report the business unit's ecological footprint and reduction progress to senior management.
- Guide team members towards greener travel choices.

**Pains:**

- **Conflicting Goals:** High pressure from upper management to reduce the unit's carbon footprint without negatively impacting business performance or client relationships.
- **Lack of Visibility:** It is currently very difficult to assess the exact environmental impact of team travel and identify the biggest "CO₂ drivers" within the unit.
- **Persuasion Hurdle:** Hard to convince employees to take longer, more sustainable routes (like trains) instead of quick flights without having clear, hard data to justify the decision.

**Gains:**

- **Clear Trade-offs:** An intuitive dashboard that instantly compares travel options (e.g., Cost vs. Time vs. CO₂ emissions), making approvals much easier.
- **Target Tracking:** A simple, visual overview showing how the business unit is performing against corporate CO₂ reduction targets.
- **Data-Driven Leadership:** Easy-to-export visuals that help communicate the "why" behind travel policies to the team and foster a more sustainable mindset.

#### **Use Case Scenario: Mark Johnson (Business Unit Manager)**

- **Context:** It is the end of the quarter, and Mark is preparing for a management meeting where he needs to report on his unit's budget and progress toward corporate CO₂ reduction targets.
- **The Action:** Mark opens the Business Travel Dashboard and filters the data specifically for his Business Unit for the current year.

**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Spotting Inefficiencies:** He immediately sees a prominent bar chart indicating that short-haul flights between Zurich and Frankfurt account for 40% of his team's total travel emissions.
- **Comparing Options:** He uses the dashboard's comparison feature to look at the alternative: traveling by train on the same route. The data clearly visualizes that while the train takes roughly 1.5 hours longer door-to-door, the CO₂ emissions are reduced by over 80%, and the financial cost is significantly lower.

**The Outcome:**
Mark exports this specific visualization from the dashboard and shares it in his next team meeting. Using the clear, undeniable data, he establishes a new internal guideline: All travel to Frankfurt must now be done by train unless there is an urgent exception. Mark is happy because he successfully reduced his unit's footprint and saved money, without needing to be a data expert to find this insight.

## Situation Assessment
Describe the available resources (data, personnel, material, software/tools, infrastructure, etc.) and time as well as restrictions and constraints. Possible risks that may arise during the project are also identified.

Dataset:
The dataset contains approcimately 25,000 business travel recorde with variables such as:
- `date` - of travel
- `transport_mode` - transport mode (e.g., flight, train, rental car, etc.)
- `km` - travel distance 
- `CO2e` - estimated CO₂ emissions
- `cost_CHF` - travel costs in Swiss Francs

Personnel:
- Jasmin Fankhauser
- Séverine Stadler
- Lia Sifrig
- Anna Kaufmann

Material: 
?

software/tools:
- Python for data exploration and visualization
- matplotlib for data visualization

Constraints and risks:
??

## Visualization Concept Lia
Translate the project objectives into a concrete visualization concept. This corresponds to the value map side of the Value Proposition Canvas [@Osterwalder2014] – describe how the proposed visualization product addresses the users' tasks, relieves their pains and creates gains. Address the following aspects:

* **Product form**: Composition of the visualization product – dashboard, data story, infographic, exploratory tool, etc.
* **Visual encodings**: Types of charts and visualizations to be used – e.g. bar chart, line chart, scatter plot, map, etc.
* **Interactivity**: Static vs. dynamic/interactive elements – filtering, drill-down, tooltips, linked views, etc.
* **Narrative and annotation**: Text elements, storytelling structure, annotations and contextual information.
* **Target medium and integration**: Embedding in the target environment – e.g. website, intranet, app, print, etc.

For each design decision, articulate the intended value along three dimensions:

* **Cognitive and analytical value**: How does the design amplify the user's ability to detect patterns, identify outliers, understand distributions or discover relationships [@card1999; @vanwijk2005]?
* **Communicative value**: How does the design support the communication of information to the target audience profile(s)? 
* **Experiential and aesthetic value**: How do the visual and interactive qualities of the design foster engagement, trust and willingness to adopt the product in practice?

Justify the concept by mapping it explicitly to the project objectives and the user needs identified in the user analysis.


## Project Plan Séverine Stadler
Divide the project into individual phases, describe them briefly and draw up a preliminary timetable, e.g. as a Gantt chart:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 5day
    section Project Understanding
        Initiation workshop and context analysis     :a1, 2024-07-01, 1d
        Stakeholder and user analysis     :a3, 2024-07-02, 5d
        Situation assessment    :a4, 2024-07-06, 1d
        Project objectives and visualization concept    :a5, 2024-07-07, 1d
        Sign project charta: milestone, m1, 2024-07-08, 1d
    
    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
        Acquire data :a6, 2024-07-08, 3d
        Exploratory data analysis   :a7, 2024-07-09, 2d
        Discuss data report: milestone, m2, 2024-07-11, 1d
        
    section Visual Encoding and Design
        Overview charts   :a8, 2024-07-11, 3d
        Maps :a9, 2024-07-11, 5d
        Implement Dashboard prototype :a10, 2024-07-16, 5d
        Complete visualization report :a10, 2024-07-21, 1d
    section Evaluation
        Prepare presentation :a10, 2024-07-22, 2d
        Project presentation : milestone, m2, 2024-07-24, 4m
```

See [Mermaid syntax for Gantt charts](https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/gantt.html). It might not be displayed correctly in Safari &#8594; use Chrome. [Live editor with export functionality](https://mermaid.live/)

## Roles and Contact Details
List the people involved in the development work here with their role titles, tasks and contact details.
